meta.title: Biblical Hebrew IPA Pronunciation Guide
meta.purpose: Machine-readable IPA-based pronunciation guide for Biblical Hebrew (Tiberian tradition). All pronunciation data is encoded in the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA) as the primary system, ensuring cross-linguistic accuracy and universality for AI-assisted language preservation.
meta.vocalization_system: Tiberian niqqud (primary); Babylonian and Palestinian traditions documented

secondary_systems.transliteration_ala_lc: American Library Association – Library of Congress

phonological_framework.description: Consonants classified by IPA place of articulation, manner of articulation, and voicing. Vowels classified by height, backness, and rounding per the IPA vowel chart.

hebrew_bible.description: The Hebrew portions of the Hebrew Bible (Tanakh), encompassing Torah, Nevi'im, and Ketuvim

canonical_divisions.name: Nevi'im (Prophets)
canonical_divisions.books: Joshua, Judges, Samuel, Kings, Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, The Twelve

canonical_divisions.name: Ketuvim (Writings)
canonical_divisions.books: Psalms, Proverbs, Job, Song of Songs, Ruth, Lamentations, Ecclesiastes, Esther, Daniel, Ezra-Nehemiah, Chronicles

notes: Biblical Hebrew is written right-to-left in Hebrew square script
The Tiberian niqqud system is the primary vocalization documented here
All IPA transcriptions use the standard 2015 IPA chart notation
Phonemic transcriptions use / / slashes; phonetic transcriptions use [ ] brackets
Suprasegmentals: /ː/ marks vowel length; /ˈ/ marks primary stress; /ˤ/ marks pharyngealization
This guide covers the same script and vocalization system as the Biblical Aramaic companion guide; the differences are primarily linguistic (morphology, phonological rules, stress patterns)

phonological_inventory.description: The complete phonemic inventory of Biblical Hebrew as vocalized in the Tiberian tradition, organized by IPA categories

consonant_phonemes.description: All consonant phonemes of Biblical Hebrew arranged by place and manner of articulation (IPA standard layout)
consonant_phonemes.note: 22 letters yield 29 distinct consonant phonemes: 16 non-Begadkephat consonants (including both Shin /ʃ/ and Sin /s/) + 6 Begadkephat hard (plosive) + 6 Begadkephat soft (fricative) + labial-velar /w/ = 29

bilabial.notes: /b/ and /v/ are allophones of Bet; /p/ and /f/ are allophones of Pe.

dental.notes: /ð/ is soft Dalet; /θ/ is soft Tav

alveolar.notes: /tˤ/ is Tet (emphatic); /sˤ/ is Tsadi (emphatic). /d/ and /ð/ are allophones of Dalet; /t/ and /θ/ are allophones of Tav.

postalveolar.notes: /ʃ/ is Shin (שׁ). Sin (שׂ) = /s/ is classified under alveolar.

velar.notes: /ɡ/ and /ɣ/ are allophones of Gimel; /k/ and /x/ are allophones of Kaf

ipa_consonant_chart.description: IPA consonant chart for Biblical Hebrew (rows = manner, columns = place). Voiceless precedes voiced in each cell (IPA convention).
ipa_consonant_chart.note: /w/ is labial-velar and not shown in this simplified chart.

vowel_phonemes.description: The vowel phonemes of Biblical Hebrew in the Tiberian tradition, arranged by the IPA vowel quadrilateral (height × backness × rounding)
vowel_phonemes.note: 7 primary vowel qualities plus 3 hataf (ultra-short) variants and schwa

ipa_vowel_chart.description: IPA vowel quadrilateral for Biblical Hebrew (Tiberian tradition)

phonemes.distribution: Short /i/ or long /iː/ (Hiriq gadol with Yod)
Close-mid front vowel; long when written plene with Yod
Qamats gadol = /ɔ/; Qamats qatan = /ɔ/ in closed unaccented syllable
Close-mid back rounded vowel; long when written plene with Vav
Close back rounded vowel; Shuruq (Vav+dagesh) for plene writing

reduced_vowels.name: Shva (mobile)
reduced_vowels.description: Mid-central vowel; occurs as shva na (mobile) in specific contexts

reduced_vowels.name: Hataf Segol
reduced_vowels.description: Ultra-short open-mid front vowel; under guttural consonants

reduced_vowels.name: Hataf Patach
reduced_vowels.description: Ultra-short open front vowel; under guttural consonants

reduced_vowels.name: Hataf Qamats
reduced_vowels.description: Ultra-short open-mid back rounded vowel; under guttural consonants

consonants.description: The 22 consonant letters of the Hebrew square script used for Biblical Hebrew, with full IPA phonological descriptions. Each letter includes place of articulation, manner of articulation, voicing, and all allophonic variants.
consonants.effective_phoneme_note: 22 letters produce 23 base consonant phonemes because Shin (ש) has two values: /ʃ/ (shin) and /s/ (sin)

letters.note: Tiberian Resh was primarily uvular /ʁ/ (the 'light' resh). An alveolar emphatic allophone [rˤ] (the 'heavy' resh) occurred adjacent to dental/alveolar consonants (ד ,ת ,ט ,צ ,ס ,ל ,נ ,ז). Resh resists dagesh forte and is sometimes grouped with gutturals in Tiberian grammar.
Single letter with two phonemic values distinguished by diacritical dot position. Shin dot (upper-right) = /ʃ/; Sin dot (upper-left) = /s/.

begadkephat.description: The six Begadkephat consonants (בגדכפת) that alternate between plosive (stop) and fricative pronunciation. In Biblical Hebrew, the plosive form is marked by Dagesh lene (U+05BC); the fricative form occurs without dagesh, typically after a vowel.

marking_system.soft_marker: Absence of dagesh (or Rafe U+05BF in some manuscripts)
marking_system.contrast_with_peshitta: Peshitta Syriac uses two separate marks: Qushaya (U+0741) for hard and Rukkakha (U+0742) for soft. Biblical Hebrew uses presence/absence of a single dagesh dot.

begadkephat.spirantization_rule: A Begadkephat letter is realized as a fricative (soft) when it immediately follows a vowel sound and does not carry dagesh forte (gemination). It is realized as a plosive (hard) word-initially, after a consonant, or when carrying dagesh.

dagesh_system.description: The dagesh is a single dot placed inside a Hebrew letter (U+05BC). It serves two entirely different phonological functions depending on context. This dual function is unique to the Hebrew/Aramaic script and has no parallel in Syriac.

dagesh_lene.name: Dagesh Lene (Dagesh Qal)
dagesh_lene.function: Marks the plosive (hard/stop) pronunciation of a Begadkephat letter
dagesh_lene.applies_to: Only the 6 Begadkephat letters: ב ג ד כ פ ת
dagesh_lene.phonological_effect: Blocks spirantization; letter is realized as plosive rather than fricative
conditions: After a major disjunctive cantillation accent (pause)

dagesh_forte.name: Dagesh Forte (Dagesh Chazaq)
dagesh_forte.applies_to: Almost any consonant letter (except gutturals אהחע and usually ר)
dagesh_forte.phonological_effect: The consonant is phonologically doubled: the coda of the preceding syllable and the onset of the following syllable
conditions: Always preceded by a vowel (never word-initial)
The preceding syllable is closed by the first half of the geminate

subtypes.conjunctive: Compensates for assimilated Nun: מִנְ + ד → מִדּ (min + d → mid.d-)
subtypes.compensatory: After short vowel when expected gemination occurs
subtypes.characteristic: Inherent in certain morphological patterns (e.g., Piel verb stem)
subtypes.article_dagesh: The Hebrew definite article הַ causes dagesh forte on the following consonant

examples.context: Definite article causes dagesh forte in Mem: the king
Piel verb stem: dagesh forte in second root letter

dagesh_system.disambiguation_rule: If the letter is a Begadkephat letter and the dagesh could be either lene or forte, determine by context: dagesh lene occurs only when no vowel precedes (word-initial or post-consonantal); dagesh forte always has a preceding vowel. In a Begadkephat letter with dagesh forte, BOTH effects apply: the letter is geminated AND realized as a plosive.

letters_that_reject_dagesh_forte.note: These letters resist gemination. When expected, compensatory vowel lengthening may occur in the preceding syllable instead.

mappiq.description: A dagesh-like dot inside final He (הּ) that indicates He is a true consonant /h/ rather than a silent mater lectionis

example.meaning: her land (the /h/ is pronounced as a consonantal suffix)

vowel_diacritics_tiberian.description: The complete Tiberian niqqud vowel system used to vocalize Biblical Hebrew texts. All vowel points are placed below or above the consonant they follow.
vowel_diacritics_tiberian.system: Tiberian (sublinear, developed by Masoretes of Tiberias c. 750–950 CE)
vowel_diacritics_tiberian.note: 13 vowel signs: 7 primary vowels + 1 shva + 3 hataf vowels + Holam Haser + Shuruq (composite)

vowels.name: Shva
vowels.note: Mobile shva = [ə]; quiescent shva = silent. See shva_system section.

vowels.name: Hataf Patach
vowels.note: Ultra-short /a/; most common hataf vowel under gutturals

vowels.name: Hiriq
vowels.note: Hiriq qatan (short) alone; Hiriq gadol (long /iː/) when followed by Yod as mater lectionis

vowels.name: Tsere
vowels.note: Close-mid front vowel; often long, especially with Yod mater lectionis

vowels.name: Segol
vowels.note: Open-mid front vowel; characteristic of segholate nouns

vowels.name: Patach
vowels.note: Open front unrounded vowel; furtive patach occurs under final guttural

vowels.name: Qamats
vowels.note: Qamats gadol = /ɔ/ (long, in open or accented syllables). Qamats qatan = /ɔ/ in closed unaccented syllables.

vowels.name: Holam
vowels.note: Close-mid back rounded vowel; Holam male (with Vav) = /oː/

vowels.name: Holam Haser
vowels.note: Holam written without Vav carrier; used when Vav would be ambiguous

vowels.name: Shuruq
vowels.note: Long /uː/ written as Vav (U+05D5) with dagesh (U+05BC). No separate codepoint.

shva_system.description: The shva (ְ, U+05B0) system is a critical feature of the Tiberian vocalization. Shva can be either mobile (na, pronounced as /ə/) or quiescent (nach, silent). Correct identification determines syllable structure and pronunciation.

shva_na.name: Shva Na (Mobile Shva)
shva_na.description: Pronounced as a short mid-central vowel [ə]; opens a new syllable

rules.rule: Word-initial shva
rules.explanation: Shva under the first consonant of a word is always mobile

rules.rule: Second of two consecutive shvas (non-final)
rules.explanation: When two shvas appear in sequence within a word, the second is mobile

rules.rule: After a long vowel
rules.explanation: Shva following a long vowel is mobile

rules.rule: Under a letter with dagesh forte
rules.explanation: Shva under a geminated consonant is mobile (it follows the second half of the geminate)

rules.rule: Under the first of two identical consonants
rules.explanation: To prevent ambiguity, the shva is mobile

shva_nach.name: Shva Nach (Quiescent Shva)
shva_nach.description: Silent; closes the preceding syllable. Does not represent any vowel sound.

rules.rule: Word-final shva
rules.explanation: Shva at the end of a word is always quiescent (silent)

rules.rule: First of two consecutive shvas (non-final)
rules.explanation: When two shvas appear in sequence, the first is quiescent (closes syllable)

rules.rule: After a short vowel in a closed syllable
rules.explanation: Shva after a short vowel closes that syllable

shva_system.guttural_exception: Guttural consonants (אהחע) do not take simple shva. Instead, they take one of the three hataf (composite) vowels: hataf segol /ɛ̆/, hataf patach /ă/, or hataf qamats /ɔ̆/. See hataf_vowels section.

hataf_vowels.description: Three ultra-short (reduced) vowels unique to the Tiberian vocalization system. They replace simple shva under guttural consonants (אהחע), which cannot take ordinary shva. Each hataf vowel preserves the quality of its corresponding full vowel but in ultra-short duration.

vowels.name: Hataf Segol
vowels.typical_contexts: Under א and ה most commonly

vowels.name: Hataf Patach
vowels.typical_contexts: Most common hataf vowel; used under all four gutturals

vowels.name: Hataf Qamats
vowels.typical_contexts: Under gutturals, especially in specific morphological patterns

shin_sin_distinction.description: The Hebrew letter Shin (ש, U+05E9) represents two distinct phonemes in Biblical Hebrew, distinguished by a diacritical dot. Sin is more common in Hebrew roots than in Biblical Aramaic.

sin.note: Sin /s/ is more common in Hebrew roots than in Aramaic. Key Hebrew examples: שָׂרָה /sɔːrɔ/ (Sarah), יִשְׂרָאֵל /jis.rɔ.ʔɛl/ (Israel)

final_form_letters.description: Five Hebrew letters have distinct glyph forms when they appear at the end of a word. These final (sofit) forms represent the SAME phoneme as their medial counterparts — the difference is purely orthographic, not phonological.

letters.name: Kaf sofit
letters.note: Final Kaf is typically spirantized [x] as it follows a vowel

letters.name: Mem sofit
letters.note: Closed rectangular form in final position

letters.name: Nun sofit
letters.note: Extended descender form in final position

letters.name: Pe sofit
letters.note: Final Pe is typically spirantized [f] as it follows a vowel

letters.name: Tsadi sofit
letters.note: Extended descender form in final position

consonant_vowel_ipa_matrix.description: IPA matrix for all consonant-vowel combinations in Biblical Hebrew. 29 consonant sounds × 11 vowels.

diacritical_marks.description: Non-vowel diacritical marks used in Biblical Hebrew texts with their Unicode codepoints and phonological functions.

marks.name: Dagesh / Mappiq
functions: Dagesh lene: marks plosive pronunciation in Begadkephat letters
Dagesh forte: marks gemination (doubling) in any eligible letter
Mappiq: in final He (הּ), marks consonantal /h/

marks.name: Rafe
marks.function: Horizontal bar indicating soft (fricative) pronunciation of Begadkephat letter; explicitly marks absence of dagesh. Rare in printed texts but found in some manuscripts.

marks.name: Shin dot
marks.function: Distinguishes Shin /ʃ/ from Sin /s/. Placed upper-right on ש.

marks.name: Sin dot
marks.function: Distinguishes Sin /s/ from Shin /ʃ/. Placed upper-left on ש.

marks.name: Meteg (Siluq)
marks.function: Marks secondary stress; prevents vowel reduction in its syllable. Also indicates that a following shva is mobile.

marks.name: Maqaf
marks.function: Hyphen joining two or more words into a single phonological unit. The joined words share one primary stress (on the last word); preceding words lose their independent stress.

marks.name: Paseq
marks.function: Vertical bar indicating a pause or separation between words. Prevents phonological merging.

marks.name: Sof Pasuq
marks.function: Colon-like mark at the end of a verse. Marks the strongest disjunctive break.

suprasegmentals.description: Prosodic features that extend beyond individual segments (consonants and vowels) in Biblical Hebrew.

gemination.description: Consonant doubling/lengthening, marked by dagesh forte (U+05BC)
gemination.ipa_notation: Geminate consonants are written as two identical IPA symbols: /bb/, /kk/, /ll/, etc.
gemination.phonological_effect: The geminate consonant closes the preceding syllable (as coda) and opens the following syllable (as onset)
gemination.restrictions: Guttural consonants (אהחע) and Resh (ר) typically resist gemination

stress.description: Biblical Hebrew has a phonologically significant stress system indicated by cantillation marks. Hebrew stress patterns are more complex and better documented than Biblical Aramaic.
stress.ipa_notation: /ˈ/ = primary stress (before stressed syllable); /ˌ/ = secondary stress

milra.definition: Ultimate (final syllable) stress — the default and most common pattern in Biblical Hebrew

milel.definition: Penultimate stress — occurs in specific morphological patterns

categories.description: Bisyllabic nouns of the pattern CeCeC

nasog_achor.definition: Stress retraction to avoid consecutive stressed syllables (stress clash avoidance)
nasog_achor.mechanism: When two words are closely connected (conjunctive accent) and both would have adjacent stresses, the first word retracts its stress to the penultimate syllable
nasog_achor.ipa_effect: Primary stress mark /ˈ/ moves one syllable earlier in the first word

waw_consecutive_stress.wayyiqtol: Waw-consecutive with imperfect (wayyiqtol): stress typically retracts to penultimate syllable
waw_consecutive_stress.weqatal: Waw-consecutive with perfect (weqatal): stress may advance to final syllable

stress.meteg_effect: Meteg (U+05BD) marks secondary stress and prevents vowel reduction
stress.maqaf_effect: Words joined by maqaf (U+05BE) form a single stress unit; only the last word retains primary stress

vowel_length.description: Tiberian vocalization distinguishes long and short vowels, though length is not always contrastive.
vowel_length.note: In open accented syllables, vowels tend to be long; in closed unaccented syllables, vowels tend to be short.

rules: Every syllable must begin with a consonant (onset is obligatory)
Open syllables (CV) typically contain long vowels
Closed syllables (CVC) typically contain short vowels
No syllable begins with a vowel; word-initial vowels have a glottal stop onset [ʔ]
Complex onsets (CC-) are resolved by epenthetic shva

pausal_forms.description: Biblical Hebrew has a systematic pattern of vowel changes at major pausal positions in the text. These pausal forms affect pronunciation significantly and have no direct parallel in Biblical Aramaic.

triggering_accents.description: Cantillation accents that trigger pausal vowel changes

primary.name: Silluq (סִלּוּק)
primary.strength: Strongest disjunctive — always triggers pausal forms

primary.name: Atnah (אַתְנָח)
primary.strength: Second strongest — almost always triggers pausal forms

secondary.name: Zaqef Qaton
secondary.note: Occasionally triggers pausal forms when it marks a major syntactic break

secondary.name: Segolta
secondary.note: Rarely triggers pausal forms; generally weaker

poetic_system.name: Ole ve-Yored (עוֹלֶה וְיוֹרֵד)
poetic_system.note: In the poetic cantillation system (Psalms, Proverbs, Job), Ole ve-Yored functions as the strongest midverse disjunctive and triggers pausal forms

vowel_shifts.description: Short open /a/ lengthens to long back /ɔː/ in pause
Short open-mid /ɛ/ raises to long close-mid /eː/ in pause
Short /i/ may lower to /eː/ in certain verb forms in pause
In pause, reduced vowels may be restored to their original full quality

morphological_patterns: Segholate nouns undergo regular pausal lengthening in the stressed syllable
Strong verb forms: qal perfect 3ms shows /a/ → /ɔː/ in final syllable
Pronominal suffixes may show pausal lengthening
Nouns with doubly-closed syllables typically do NOT have separate pausal forms
pausal_forms.note: Pausal forms are a defining feature of Biblical Hebrew prosody. When extracting sample words from verse-final or atnah positions, the vowels may differ from their contextual (non-pausal) forms.

definite_article.description: Biblical Hebrew uses a prefixed definite article הַ (ha-) that assimilates with the following consonant via dagesh forte. This is a Hebrew-specific feature; Biblical Aramaic uses a suffixed emphatic state (-א) instead.

basic_form.rule: The article prefix הַ is added to the noun, and dagesh forte is placed in the following consonant to indicate gemination

rules.name: Before gutturals (א ע) and Resh (ר)
rules.condition: These letters cannot take dagesh forte

rules.name: Before ה and unaccented ח/ע with qamats
rules.condition: Before He, or before Chet/Ayin when unaccented and followed by qamats
rules.note: Before unaccented ח and ע with qamats, the article takes segol /ɛ/ instead of patach

rules.name: Before accented ח and ה
rules.condition: Before Chet or He when accented

definite_article.contrast_with_aramaic: Biblical Aramaic expresses definiteness through the emphatic state suffix /-ɔː/ (written -א), e.g., מַלְכָּא /mal.kɔː/ (the king). Hebrew uses a prefix; Aramaic uses a suffix.

cantillation_overview.description: Overview of the te'amim (cantillation marks) and their prosodic effects on pronunciation. Biblical Hebrew has TWO cantillation systems: one for the 21 prose books and one for the 3 poetic books (Psalms, Proverbs, Job — the 'emet' books).
cantillation_overview.relevance_to_pronunciation: Cantillation marks indicate: (1) which syllable receives primary stress, (2) where pauses occur (affecting Begadkephat spirantization and pausal forms), (3) phrase grouping (affecting connected speech).

prose_system.name: Prose cantillation (21 books)
prose_system.applies_to: All books of the Hebrew Bible except Psalms, Proverbs, and Job

disjunctive.name: Disjunctive accents (Melakhim / Mafsikim)
disjunctive.function: Create pauses/breaks of varying strength between phrases

hierarchy.name: Silluq + Sof Pasuq / Atnah
hierarchy.function: Verse division: Silluq marks the last stressed word; Atnah divides the verse into two halves

disjunctive.phonological_effect: After a strong disjunctive pause, the next word's initial Begadkephat letter is realized as a plosive (as if word-initial), because the pause breaks the vowel-to-consonant connection.

conjunctive.name: Conjunctive accents (Meshartim)
conjunctive.function: Link words together within a phrase
conjunctive.phonological_effect: Words connected by conjunctive accents form a tight phonological unit; spirantization applies across word boundaries.

poetic_system.name: Poetic cantillation (3 books: Psalms, Proverbs, Job)
poetic_system.applies_to: The three 'emet' (אמת) books: Psalms (תהלים), Proverbs (משׁלי), Job (איוב)
poetic_system.description: A distinct cantillation system with different accent names, different hierarchical structure, and different melodic traditions from the prose system.

disjunctive.name: Disjunctive accents (Poetic)

hierarchy.name: Silluq + Sof Pasuq / Ole ve-Yored / Atnah
hierarchy.function: Primary verse divisions. Ole ve-Yored is the highest-ranking midverse disjunctive in the poetic system.

hierarchy.name: Revia Mugrash / Revia Gadol / Zaqef Qaton

disjunctive.phonological_effect: Same as prose: disjunctive pauses affect spirantization and may trigger pausal forms.

conjunctive.name: Conjunctive accents (Poetic)
conjunctive.phonological_effect: Same as prose: connected words form phonological units.

key_differences_from_prose: Ole ve-Yored replaces Zaqef as the primary midverse disjunctive
Revia Mugrash is unique to the poetic system
The hierarchy of disjunctive strength differs from the prose order
Fewer distinct accent marks overall but more flexible phrase structure
Atnah appears less frequently; some verses lack it entirely

cantillation_overview.note: A full cantillation guide is beyond the scope of this pronunciation reference. This section documents only the prosodic/phonological effects relevant to pronunciation.

phonological_rules.description: Systematic phonological processes that apply in Biblical Hebrew pronunciation.

rules.name: Begadkephat spirantization
rules.rule: /b d g k p t/ → [v ð ɣ x f θ] after a vowel (when no dagesh is present)
rules.note: The most important allophonic rule. Does not apply word-initially or after a consonant.

rules.name: Nun assimilation
rules.rule: /n/ → ∅ / __ C (with gemination of following consonant)
rules.formal: /n/ → Ci / __ Ci (where Ci = following consonant, which geminates)

example.note: Nun assimilates to following consonant, shown by dagesh forte: מִדְּ

rules.name: Vowel reduction (shva)
rules.rule: Full vowels reduce to /ə/ in pretonic open syllables under certain morphological conditions
rules.note: This is a morphophonological process tied to word formation.

rules.name: Guttural vowel coloring
rules.rule: Gutturals prefer /a/-colored vowels and take hataf vowels instead of simple shva
rules.note: Guttural consonants also resist gemination (dagesh forte).

rules.name: Furtive patach
rules.rule: A short /a/ (patach) is inserted BEFORE a final guttural (ח ע הּ) when preceded by a non-/a/ vowel
rules.note: Written after the guttural in the script but pronounced BEFORE it: רוּחַ = /ruː.aħ/ not */ruː.ħa/

rules.name: Emphatic spreading
rules.rule: Emphatic (pharyngealized) consonants spread their [+RTR] (retracted tongue root) feature to adjacent vowels
rules.note: Vowels adjacent to /tˤ/, /sˤ/, /q/ may be backed or lowered phonetically.

rules.name: Word-boundary spirantization
rules.rule: A Begadkephat letter at the start of a word following a vowel-final word (without intervening pause) may spirantize
rules.exception: A strong disjunctive cantillation accent on the preceding word creates a pause, blocking cross-word spirantization.

rules.name: Compensatory lengthening
rules.rule: When a guttural rejects dagesh forte (gemination), the preceding vowel may lengthen to compensate
rules.example: Definite article before guttural: הַ + אִישׁ → הָאִישׁ /hɔːʔiʃ/

rules.name: Pausal lengthening
rules.rule: At major pausal positions (silluq, atnah), certain vowels undergo systematic lengthening
rules.note: See pausal_forms section for full details.

rules.name: Definite article assimilation
rules.rule: The article prefix הַ causes dagesh forte on the following consonant, with compensatory lengthening before gutturals and Resh
rules.note: See definite_article section for full rules.

rules.name: Nasog achor (stress retraction)
rules.rule: When two consecutive words would have adjacent stresses, the first word's stress retracts to avoid clash
rules.formal: ˈσˈσ → ˈσσ + ˈσ (stress moves left in first word)
rules.note: More productive in Hebrew than in Aramaic.

rules.name: Waw-consecutive effects
rules.rule: Waw-consecutive wayyiqtol causes dagesh forte in the following consonant and may retract stress; weqatal may advance stress to final syllable

rules.name: Qamats qatan identification
rules.rule: Qamats in a closed, unaccented syllable is qamats qatan /ɔ/ rather than qamats gadol /ɔː/
key_patterns: חָכְמָה /ħɔx.mɔː/ — first syllable closed by shva nach = qamats qatan

vocalization_traditions.description: Three distinct vocalization traditions developed for marking vowels in Hebrew texts. This guide primarily documents the Tiberian tradition (standard), with comparative notes on the Babylonian and Palestinian systems. The Tiberian tradition is most thoroughly studied and documented for Biblical Hebrew.

traditions.name: Tiberian
traditions.developers: Ben Asher and Ben Naphtali Masoretic families
traditions.status: Canonical standard. All modern printed Hebrew Bibles use this system. The Aleppo Codex and Leningrad Codex represent the Ben Asher tradition.

traditions.name: Babylonian
key_differences_from_tiberian: Fewer vowel distinctions: no consistent /e/ vs /ɛ/ contrast
Marks placed above rather than below letters
Preserved in some Yemenite reading traditions
traditions.status: Supplanted by Tiberian system. Preserved in some Yemenite traditions and early manuscript fragments.

traditions.name: Palestinian
key_differences_from_tiberian: Five-vowel system: no /e/ vs /ɛ/ contrast, no /ɔ/ vs /o/ contrast
traditions.status: Most communities followed Palestinian pronunciation while writing Tiberian signs.

vocalization_traditions.practical_note: The IPA values in this guide follow the Tiberian tradition as the standard reference. Users working with Babylonian or Palestinian vocalization manuscripts should apply the simplified vowel inventories listed above.

cross_reference_to_aramaic.description: Systematic three-way mapping of phonological correspondences between Biblical Hebrew (this guide), Biblical Aramaic, and Peshitta Syriac. All three represent closely related Semitic languages sharing the same consonant inventory.
shared_features: Same 22-consonant inventory with identical IPA values across all three
Same Begadkephat spirantization pattern (same 6 consonant pairs, same IPA values)

differences.biblical_hebrew: Tiberian niqqud: 7 primary vowels + shva + 3 hataf = 11 vowel signs
differences.peshitta_syriac: Eastern (7 vowels, dot-based) or Western (5 vowels, Greek-derived)

differences.biblical_hebrew: שׁ /ʃ/ vs שׂ /s/ — Sin more common in Hebrew roots
differences.peshitta_syriac: ܨ /ʃ/ only — Syriac Sin is separate letter Semkath ܣ /s/

differences.biblical_hebrew: Dagesh (U+05BC) = single mark; presence = plosive, absence = fricative
differences.peshitta_syriac: Qushaya (U+0741) = hard; Rukkakha (U+0742) = soft; two explicit marks

differences.biblical_hebrew: Prefix הַ (ha-) with dagesh forte on following consonant
Systematic vowel changes at silluq/atnah positions
Complex: milra (default), mil'el (segholates, etc.), nasog achor
differences.biblical_aramaic: Predominantly milra with fewer mil'el exceptions
differences.peshitta_syriac: Penultimate stress in Eastern; final stress in Western

differences.biblical_hebrew: Two systems: prose (21 books) + poetic (Psalms, Proverbs, Job)
differences.biblical_aramaic: Prose system only (Aramaic portions are in prose books)

differences.biblical_hebrew: Wayyiqtol/weqatal with stress shifts and dagesh forte

consonant_ipa_correspondence.note: The following table confirms that all 22 consonant IPA values are IDENTICAL across Biblical Hebrew, Biblical Aramaic, and Peshitta Syriac.

sample_words_ipa.description: Sample words drawn from ACTUAL Biblical Hebrew texts with full IPA transcription, segmental breakdown, and syllabification. Each word is byte-identical to its source file. Segments are derived by slicing the actual word codepoints, not independently typed.
sample_words_ipa.source_note: All sample words are taken from the Hebrew portions of the Hebrew Bible as preserved in the workspace Niqqud/ folder. Words were chosen to showcase diverse phonological features: Begadkephat alternation, shva mobile/quiescent, hataf vowels, shin vs sin, dagesh lene vs forte, pausal forms, definite article, qamats qatan vs gadol.

features_demonstrated: Begadkephat: Bet with dagesh lene → plosive /b/

segments.note: Alef is quiescent (silent) here; etymological spelling

features_demonstrated: Definite article הַ with dagesh forte on following consonant
Bet without dagesh (after vowel) → fricative /v/ (Begadkephat spirantization)
Definite article before Alef (guttural) → compensatory lengthening: patach→qamats
Bet with dagesh forte → gemination /bb/ (doubled consonant)
Final yod as mater lectionis (silent, extends /i/)
Bet with dagesh lene (after prefix shva) → plosive /b/ (Begadkephat)
Qamats qatan → /ɔ/ (NOT gadol /ɔː/; in closed unaccented syllable before /l/)
Alef with hataf segol → /ʔɛ̆/ (ultra-short, guttural preference)

unicode_reference.description: Complete Unicode reference for the Hebrew block (U+0590–U+05FF) as used in Biblical Hebrew texts.

numerical_values.description: Gematria — the traditional numerical values assigned to each Hebrew letter. Values are identical across Biblical Hebrew, Biblical Aramaic, and Peshitta Syriac.

diphthongs.description: Diphthongs in Biblical Hebrew — vowel sequences that function as single phonological units within a syllable.

diphthongs.note: May monophthongize to /eː/ in some environments
Common in demonstratives and plural endings
Diphthongs are identical across Biblical Hebrew, Biblical Aramaic, and Peshitta Syriac, with similar monophthongization patterns.
